column1: SPEC Template — NEURONIUM AI (Commitment-aware AI Super Agent)

column2: Fill In
NEURONIUM AI — Commitment-aware AI Super Agent with Action Graph (DAG) planning, hybrid memory (GraphRAG + agentic retrieval), verification critics, typed contracts, and audit/replay trace

Build an open-source Python framework that converts a high-level user objective into a Hierarchical Task Network (HTN), compiles it into a deterministic Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) of typed nodes (ModelNode/CodeNode/McpToolNode), executes it with pause/stop/revise/replan control, persists every step into content-addressable storage and an index (SQLite/Postgres+pgvector), records a canonical trace, validates outputs through critic verifiers, and supports full offline replay. Scope covers: public Python API (AgentRunner), CLI (neuronium-agent), TOML configuration, FS CAS / SQLite local mode, Postgres + Redis + RQ production mode, MCP tool integration, GraphRAG-lite memory, Docker code sandbox.

Dataism Lab (open-source community); target adopters — AI engineering teams in mid/large enterprises, R&D labs, regulated industries (fintech, medtech, govtech) and AI agent platform builders that need auditable and reproducible agent execution

Modern LLM-agent frameworks (LangChain, AutoGen, CrewAI) are non-deterministic, hard to audit, opaque under failure, lack reproducible replay, mix planning with execution, and have no first-class commitment/control protocol. This blocks enterprise adoption where SLA, compliance, root-cause analysis and reproducibility are mandatory.

A layered framework that (a) separates Plan → Execute → Verify → Adapt, (b) compiles plans into typed DAGs with canonical JSON for byte-identical replay, (c) records every transition into an append-only trace with content-addressed artifacts, (d) exposes a declarative control protocol (continue/pause/revise/replan/stop/escalate) over a state machine, (e) provides critic-based verification before commit, (f) allows scaling local single-process runs to distributed Redis+RQ workers without code change.

1) Replay determinism rate ≥ 99% on identical seed/config (byte-equality of artifact_id). 2) Mean time to resume from PAUSED checkpoint ≤ 2s (local) / 10s (distributed). 3) Trace coverage = 100% of state transitions and node executions persisted. 4) Critic precision/recall ≥ 0.9 on benchmark verification suite. 5) Time-to-onboard new node type by 3rd-party developer ≤ 1 day. 6) p95 end-to-end latency for ≤10-node DAG ≤ 30s with gpt-4.1-mini. 7) Zero data loss on graceful pause/stop (all checkpoints flushed). 8) Memory recall@k ≥ 0.85 on retrieval benchmark with k=10. 9) Custom critic plug-in registration time ≤ 30 min (from skeleton to integration test green). 10) MCP tool round-trip overhead ≤ 100 ms p95 excluding network. 11) Worker failover (lease expiry → re-claim) ≤ 30 s with no duplicate node execution. 12) Observability fan-out latency from event emit to listener ≤ 50 ms p95.
AI Engineer / Agent Operator
US-1

As an AI Engineer, I want to launch a deterministic AI-agent run from an objective and interactively control it (pause / resume / stop / revise), so that I can ship reliable agents without losing work on interruptions and without re-running expensive LLM calls.

1) Engineer writes neuronium.toml and exports NEURONIUM_OPENAI_API_KEY. 2) Runs `neuronium-agent run -o "<objective>" --interactive`. 3) CLI prints a human-readable timeline (plan → nodes → critics). 4) Engineer presses keys to send pause/stop or types a revise payload. 5) On pause, a checkpoint is written; on resume, run continues from latest phase boundary. 6) Engineer inspects rendered HTML artifact and the run summary.
A valid neuronium.toml exists, NEURONIUM_OPENAI_API_KEY is set, blob backend = fs_cas and index backend = sqlite are reachable, no run with the same execution_id is active.
The user invokes `neuronium-agent run -o "<objective>"` (or calls AgentRunner.start(RunRequest(objective=...))) and the orchestrator transitions PENDING → RUNNING.

A RunHandle (trace_id, execution_id, created_at) is returned, the HTN planner produces a DAG, nodes execute in topological order, every transition is appended to the trace, artifacts are written into FS CAS by canonical artifact_id, and the run terminates in COMPLETED with progress=1.0; if any node fails non-retryably the run terminates in FAILED with FailureClass recorded.
RunRequest{ objective: str, constraints: list[str]=[], mode: "batch"|"supervised"|"interactive"|None, metadata: dict={} }; AppConfig loaded from neuronium.toml; environment variables.
RunHandle, RunStatus(state=COMPLETED|FAILED, progress, current_node_ref, message), trace events in index, rendered HTML artifact path, content-addressed artifacts in CAS.
Run state machine: PENDING → RUNNING → (PAUSED ⇄ RUNNING)* → COMPLETED|FAILED|CANCELLED. Each node: PENDING → READY → RUNNING → COMPLETED|FAILED|TIMEOUT|RETRYING|CANCELLED.
Requirement
The system SHALL accept a RunRequest and return a RunHandle synchronously containing trace_id and execution_id generated as canonical UUIDv7-style identifiers.

The HTN planner SHALL convert the objective + constraints into an Action Graph (DAG) of typed nodes (ModelNode, CodeNode, McpToolNode) with declared inputs/outputs and no cycles, validated before execution.

The deterministic executor SHALL execute nodes in valid topological order, respect llm_temperature=0.0 and default_random_seed from determinism config, and persist each transition (NODE_STARTED, NODE_COMPLETED, NODE_FAILED) into the trace store.
Requirement
Determinism: identical (config, seed, inputs) MUST yield identical canonical artifact_id values across runs (byte-equality), targeting ≥ 99% on the test corpus.
Performance: orchestrator overhead per node transition SHALL be ≤ 50 ms p95 on local SQLite/FS CAS (excluding LLM and tool latency).
Reliability: trace and CAS writes SHALL be crash-safe (fsync on commit), so a SIGKILL never produces a partially indexed artifact or torn trace event.
Fill In
A run is in state RUNNING with trace_id T and at least one node has reached a phase boundary checkpoint.

The operator sends ControlCommand(type ∈ {pause, stop, revise, replan, continue, escalate}, payload) via AgentRunner.control(handle, cmd) or interactive CLI keystroke; in the pause/stop case an InterruptRequest(mode="graceful"|"immediate") may be supplied.

The orchestrator performs declarative state-transition → checkpoint → trace decision WITHOUT executing further DAG nodes; on pause, latest resume_context is persisted and exposed via get_latest_pause_context; on continue/resume_run, execution restarts from the latest phase-boundary checkpoint with no duplicate side-effects; on revise/replan, the planner regenerates the affected sub-DAG and appends a CONTROL_APPLIED event.
ControlCommand{ type, payload: dict }, optional InterruptRequest{ command, mode, export_path }; existing RunHandle.
Updated RunStatus reflecting new state (PAUSED/RUNNING/CANCELLED), CONTROL_APPLIED trace event, optionally exported partial trace at export_path, resume_context dict on pause.

Allowed transitions: RUNNING → PAUSED (pause), PAUSED → RUNNING (continue/resume_run), RUNNING|PAUSED → CANCELLED (stop), RUNNING → RUNNING with new sub-DAG (revise/replan), any → ESCALATED on escalate. Invalid transitions are rejected with a typed error.
Requirement
The Orchestrator.apply_control method SHALL be idempotent: replaying the same ControlCommand on the same checkpoint MUST produce the same state and trace decision.
resume_run(trace_id) SHALL restart execution from the last successfully written phase-boundary checkpoint and SHALL NOT re-execute already-COMPLETED nodes.
Requirement
Control latency: time from control() call to RunStatus reflecting new state SHALL be ≤ 500 ms p95 on local backends.
Safety: no control command SHALL ever cause data loss; on graceful pause/stop all in-flight checkpoints MUST be flushed before state transition.
Auditor / Compliance Officer / QA Engineer
US-2

As an Auditor, I want to export and deterministically replay any past agent run from its trace, so that I can verify the agent's decisions, reproduce incidents, and produce evidence for compliance reviews.

1) Auditor receives a trace_id from production. 2) Runs `neuronium-agent` Python API: AgentRunner.export_trace(handle, "jsonl", "trace.jsonl") to obtain a portable trace bundle. 3) Imports the trace into an analysis env (Postgres/SQLite). 4) Calls AgentRunner.replay(trace_id) in strict offline mode → re-executes nodes against recorded artifacts only (no live LLM/tool calls). 5) Compares replay artifacts to original by artifact_id; mismatches are flagged. 6) Reads rendered HTML and critic evidence to assemble an audit report.
A completed (or failed) run with trace_id T exists in the index store, all referenced artifacts are present in CAS, the auditor has read access to both stores.
The auditor calls AgentRunner.export_trace(handle, format ∈ {"jsonl", "json", "zip"}, path) or `neuronium-agent run --trace-id T --export-trace path`.

An ordered, append-only trace file is produced at `path` containing every state transition, node start/finish, control event, critic verdict and artifact_id reference; for "zip" the bundle additionally embeds the referenced artifact bytes; checksum/manifest is included so any third party can re-import the bundle without access to the source store.
RunHandle or trace_id, TraceExportFormat, target file path, optional include-artifacts flag (implicit for zip).
A trace file (jsonl/json/zip) on disk; success boolean; manifest with event count, artifact count, sha256 digests.
Source store remains read-only; no run state changes; export operation appends an EXPORT_PERFORMED audit event referencing the export digest.
Requirement
export_trace SHALL emit events in deterministic chronological order keyed by (trace_id, sequence_no) with no gaps and no reordering.
The exported bundle SHALL be self-describing: include a manifest with neuronium-v1 canonical-json schema id, event counts, and per-artifact sha256 digests so the bundle is verifiable offline.
Re-importing an exported bundle into an empty store SHALL recreate a queryable run that passes the same get_trace_events / read_artifact_json calls byte-for-byte.
Requirement
Throughput: export of a 10k-event trace SHALL complete in ≤ 10s p95 on local SSD.
Integrity: every exported artifact reference SHALL match its sha256-based artifact_id; any mismatch MUST abort the export with a typed error.
Fill In
A trace_id T is available in the local store (or imported from a bundle) and all referenced artifacts are reachable via the configured BlobStore.
The auditor calls AgentRunner.replay(trace_id=T) which runs the executor in strict offline mode.

The DAG is re-executed using recorded inputs only; every ModelNode/McpToolNode SHALL be served from the recorded artifact instead of a live call; each newly produced artifact_id is compared against the recorded one; the run finishes in COMPLETED with a replay_report listing matches, mismatches and any missing artifacts; on any mismatch the run is marked REPLAY_DIVERGED for investigation.
trace_id, optional replay options (e.g., compare_mode = strict|loose).
RunHandle for the replay run, replay_report{ matched: int, diverged: list[node_ref], missing: list[artifact_id] }, new trace events tagged origin="replay".
A new replay run is created with state RUNNING → COMPLETED|FAILED; original run is untouched; replay events are appended with parent_trace_id pointing to T.
Requirement
Strict replay mode SHALL block any outbound network call (LLM, MCP, web) and SHALL raise NetworkAttemptInReplayError if a node attempts one without a recorded artifact.
The replay engine SHALL surface a structured replay_report and SHALL classify divergences as TRANSIENT (timestamp-only), PERSISTENT (output bytes differ), or CRITICAL (DAG topology differs).
Requirement
Reproducibility: ≥ 99% of recorded runs in the regression suite SHALL replay byte-identical given identical config and CAS contents.
Isolation: replay SHALL run in a sandboxed process with no write access to the production index store; only a dedicated replay_index is mutated.
US-3

As a Platform / Backend Developer, I want to deploy Neuronium in production with Postgres + Redis + RQ workers and pluggable critics/MCP tools, so that I can run many agent jobs concurrently with horizontal scalability, persistent storage, and policy-grade verification.

1) DevOps provisions Postgres (with pgvector) and Redis. 2) Updates neuronium.toml: storage.index_backend="postgres", queue=redis, llm.provider, optional embeddings. 3) Runs `pip install -e ".[all]"` on worker images. 4) Starts API/control plane using AgentRunner; starts N workers with `neuronium-agent worker`. 5) Submits jobs through Python API or CLI; jobs are enqueued to RQ; workers pick them, execute DAG, write to shared CAS+Postgres. 6) Developer plugs in custom MCP tools and custom critics, configures recovery policies, and monitors progress via trace_event_listener / dashboards.

Postgres ≥14 with pgvector, Redis ≥6, shared blob storage (FS or object) are reachable; neuronium.toml points to them; Docker is available for CodeNode sandbox; required MCP tool servers are running and authenticated.

An operator deploys ≥1 control-plane process and ≥N worker processes (`neuronium-agent worker`), then submits RunRequests at rate R via the public API; workers concurrently consume RQ jobs and execute DAG nodes; custom critics validate outputs; recovery policies handle TRANSIENT failures.

The system SHALL distribute work without duplicate execution (RQ job dedupe by execution_id), persist all artifacts/trace into the shared backends, isolate CodeNode execution in a Docker sandbox, surface health and progress via trace_event_listener, and degrade gracefully (DLQ + recovery policy) on PERSISTENT failures while keeping CRITICAL failures from cascading across runs.
neuronium.toml with storage=postgres + queue=redis, env vars (DB URL, REDIS_URL, OPENAI_API_KEY), optional MCP tool registry, optional custom critic and recovery-policy modules.
Per-job RunHandle, persisted runs/artifacts/trace_events in Postgres, indexed embeddings (if pgvector enabled), worker logs/metrics, rendered HTML artifacts, RQ job statuses.

Cluster-wide: each run owned by exactly one worker at a time (lease-based); Postgres tables (runs, artifacts, trace_events, artifact_index, embeddings) updated atomically per phase boundary; Redis holds queues + leases.
Requirement

The system SHALL support pluggable backends behind two interfaces (BlobStore, IndexStore) with at minimum: blob = fs_cas; index = sqlite, postgres; selection driven by neuronium.toml without code changes.

The system SHALL provide a worker entry point (`neuronium-agent worker`) that consumes RQ jobs, leases runs by execution_id, executes the DAG, and releases the lease on terminal state; orphaned leases MUST expire and be re-claimable.
Requirement
Scalability: linear throughput up to 32 concurrent workers on the standard benchmark DAG (≥ 0.85 scaling efficiency); no global locks on the hot path.

Security: CodeNode SHALL execute in a Docker sandbox with no host network and read-only mounts of approved inputs; secrets (API keys) are read from env/secret store and never persisted into trace or artifacts.
Fill In

The platform is deployed (Postgres + Redis + ≥1 worker), trace_event_listener subscribers (Prometheus exporter / Loki shipper / OpenTelemetry adapter) are registered through create_runner(trace_event_listener=...), the SRE has access to dashboards.

Runs execute under load and emit trace events; the SRE queries dashboards for throughput, p95 latency, error rate, queue depth, lease age, critic FAIL rate, replay divergence rate; alerts fire on threshold breach (e.g., queue_depth > N, lease_age > 30s, critic_fail_rate > 5%).

Every emitted trace event SHALL reach all registered listeners in order with ≤ 50 ms p95 fan-out latency; metrics SHALL include per-run, per-node-type and per-worker labels; structured logs SHALL include trace_id, execution_id, node_ref correlation IDs; alert payload SHALL contain the offending trace_id so the operator can pivot to the run timeline in one click.
Listener callable Callable[[dict[str,Any]], None] passed to create_runner; alerting thresholds configured externally (Prometheus rules / Grafana / OpsGenie).
Stream of trace event dicts to listeners; metrics scraped from /metrics endpoint (or pushed); alerts; correlated structured logs.
Listener registration is in-memory per process; no central state mutated; alerting state lives in the external monitoring system.
Requirement

The system SHALL allow registration of one or more trace_event_listener callables via create_runner; events MUST be delivered in the same order they are persisted to the trace store, with at-least-once semantics per listener.
Structured logs SHALL include correlation fields trace_id, execution_id, node_ref, control_command_id (when applicable) on every line; default formatter SHALL be JSON-line.
Requirement
Observability fan-out: time from event persisted to all listeners invoked SHALL be ≤ 50 ms p95 with up to 8 listeners.
A slow or failing listener MUST NOT block executor progress; failures SHALL be logged and isolated, and a circuit-breaker SHALL drop the listener after N consecutive failures with an alert.
Knowledge / ML Engineer
US-4

As a Knowledge Engineer, I want to ingest documents and prior-run artifacts into the GraphRAG-lite memory and let the agent retrieve them during DAG execution, so that agents can reuse organizational knowledge without re-running expensive computations.

1) Engineer prepares a corpus (markdown / PDFs / JSON / prior trace bundles). 2) Calls memory.ingest(source) which chunks → embeds → writes MemoryNode + MemoryEdge + vector(pgvector) rows. 3) Verifies via memory.search(query, k) returns relevant chunks with scores. 4) Configures a node in the DAG to call agentic retrieval (memory.retrieve(query, k, hops)). 5) During execution the agent fetches relevant context, includes it in the LLM prompt, and emits a MEMORY_HIT trace event with retrieved IDs.
A configured embeddings provider (sentence-transformers or OpenAI), an IndexStore that supports pgvector (or sqlite-vec fallback), write access, and a source corpus.

Engineer calls memory.ingest(source, kind, metadata) which chunks the source, computes embeddings, writes MemoryNode rows with content_artifact_id (CAS) + embedding vectors and MemoryEdge rows for explicit relations.

Each chunk SHALL be addressable by canonical MemoryNode.id, the embedding vector dimension SHALL match the configured model, vector index SHALL be updated transactionally, and a MEMORY_INGESTED trace event SHALL be emitted with counts and source digest; duplicate ingest of the same source SHALL be idempotent (no duplicate MemoryNode rows by content sha256).
source (path / URL / bytes / trace_id), kind (Literal["doc","artifact","trace"]), metadata dict, optional chunking config (size, overlap), optional explicit edges list.
IngestReport{ nodes_created, edges_created, embeddings_written, source_digest, duration_ms }; MEMORY_INGESTED trace event.
IndexStore: MemoryNode + MemoryEdge + embedding rows added (or skipped if duplicate); BlobStore: chunk content stored in CAS by content sha256.
Requirement
memory.ingest SHALL chunk, embed and persist with deduplication by content sha256, and SHALL emit a MEMORY_INGESTED trace event including source_digest and counts.
Embedding writes SHALL be transactional with MemoryNode/MemoryEdge inserts; partial failures MUST roll back to prevent dangling vectors.
Requirement
Throughput: ingest of 1k chunks (~1 MB total) SHALL complete in ≤ 30 s p95 with default embeddings model on a 4-vCPU host.
Fill In
Memory store has been ingested (UC-4.1 ran successfully), embeddings are available, and a node in the DAG declares a retrieval step with parameters (query, k, hops, filter).

The executor invokes memory.retrieve(query, k, hops, filter) during node execution; the system performs vector kNN search over MemoryNode embeddings, then expands by graph walk over MemoryEdge up to `hops`, deduplicates and ranks by score.

The retrieval SHALL return up to k+graph-expansion results as RetrievalResult objects with score, source, and content_artifact_id; a MEMORY_HIT trace event SHALL be emitted with the retrieved IDs and scores so replay can reproduce identical context; if no results pass the score threshold the node SHALL be served an empty context and a MEMORY_MISS event SHALL be emitted.
query (str), k (int, default 10), hops (int, default 1), filter (dict, e.g., kind="doc"), score_threshold (float, optional).
list[RetrievalResult{ id, score, content_artifact_id, source, hops }]; MEMORY_HIT or MEMORY_MISS trace event.
IndexStore: read-only vector + graph query; trace store: append MEMORY_HIT/MISS event with retrieved IDs (so replay can reproduce identical context deterministically).
Requirement
memory.retrieve SHALL combine vector kNN search with graph expansion over MemoryEdge up to `hops`, returning results deduplicated and ranked by combined score.

Every retrieval call SHALL emit a MEMORY_HIT (or MEMORY_MISS) trace event recording the resolved MemoryNode IDs so that replay re-executes the node with identical context regardless of changes to the live memory store.
Requirement
Latency: retrieval p95 ≤ 200 ms for k=10, hops=1 over a 100k-node memory store on Postgres+pgvector with appropriate indexes.
Quality: recall@10 ≥ 0.85 on the standard retrieval benchmark dataset.
Domain Expert / QA Engineer
US-5

As a Domain Expert, I want to define and register custom critic verifiers and have the agent reject and revise failing node outputs automatically, so that domain-specific business rules are enforced before any side-effect is committed.

1) Expert writes a Python class implementing CriticProtocol.verify(node_ref, output, context) → Verdict. 2) Registers it via critics.register("billing-policy", BillingCritic) or via entry-points in setup.cfg. 3) Configures neuronium.toml [verification] critics = ["demo","generic","billing-policy"]. 4) Runs an agent: each node output is gated by all matching critics; on PASS the artifact is committed; on FAIL the node is rejected, evidence stored, and the orchestrator triggers revise/replan automatically (or escalates depending on policy). 5) Expert inspects CriticVerdict rows + evidence artifact for tuning.

A Python module providing a class that implements CriticProtocol (verify(node_ref, output, context) → Verdict{result: PASS|FAIL|WARN, evidence_artifact_id, message}); the module is importable in the worker environment.

The expert registers it via critics.register(name, cls) at process start, OR declares it under [verification.critics] in neuronium.toml, OR exposes it through Python entry-point group "neuronium_agent.critics".

The orchestrator SHALL discover the critic at startup, validate that it implements CriticProtocol, include it in the gate chain for matching node types, and call verify() on every output of those nodes; verdicts SHALL be persisted to the CriticVerdict table with evidence_artifact_id, and a CRITIC_VERDICT trace event SHALL be emitted with name, result and evidence pointer.
critic class implementing CriticProtocol; registration name (str); optional match config (node_type, tag, run.metadata filter); optional priority (int, default 100).
registration receipt; subsequent CRITIC_VERDICT trace events; CriticVerdict rows with evidence; aggregated critic_pass_rate metric.
In-memory critic registry per process; verdict rows in IndexStore; evidence blob in CAS.
Requirement

The system SHALL discover critics from three sources at startup (programmatic register, neuronium.toml, entry-points) and SHALL fail fast on any class that does not implement CriticProtocol or has a duplicate name.

Every node output SHALL be passed through all matching critics in priority order before the artifact is marked committed; on the first FAIL the node SHALL be marked rejected and downstream execution SHALL be blocked.
Requirement
Critic gate overhead SHALL be ≤ 20 ms p95 per critic per node (excluding the critic's own logic), and the gate chain SHALL run in-process to avoid extra hops.
Fill In

A run is RUNNING; a node has just produced an output; one or more registered critics return Verdict.result = FAIL with evidence; recovery policy declares revise_on_critic_fail = true with a max_revise_attempts limit.

The orchestrator receives the FAIL verdict and applies the policy, automatically issuing an internal ControlCommand(type="revise", payload={node_ref, evidence_artifact_id, attempt}); the planner regenerates the affected sub-DAG with the evidence as additional context; the executor re-runs the revised sub-DAG.

The original failed artifact SHALL be retained for audit (not deleted), a CRITIC_REJECTED + CONTROL_APPLIED + REVISE_TRIGGERED event chain SHALL be appended; the new attempt counter SHALL increment; if max_revise_attempts is exceeded the run SHALL transition to ESCALATED (or FAILED if no escalation handler) with a REVISE_GIVE_UP event; on a subsequent PASS the run continues with the revised artifact committed.
FAIL Verdict from critic, recovery policy config (revise_on_critic_fail, max_revise_attempts, escalation_target); current node + DAG state.

REVISE_TRIGGERED / CRITIC_REJECTED / REVISE_GIVE_UP / ESCALATED trace events; new attempt nodes in the DAG; updated RunStatus; final committed artifact (post-revise) or terminal ESCALATED/FAILED state.
Run state stays RUNNING during revise; node attempts are tracked as separate node_ref attempts with an attempt index; counter resets on escalate/restart.
Requirement
On critic FAIL, the orchestrator SHALL automatically apply the configured recovery policy (retry / revise / escalate) without requiring an external operator if revise_on_critic_fail is enabled.
Failed artifacts and their evidence SHALL be retained, queryable by node_ref and attempt index, and exportable so QA can analyze the failure trail offline.
Requirement
Convergence: on the QA benchmark suite, ≥ 80% of revisable failures SHALL converge to PASS within max_revise_attempts ≤ 3.
Tool / Plugin Developer
US-6

As a Tool/Plugin Developer, I want to register external MCP tools and ship custom node types, critics and recovery policies as installable plugins, so that I can extend Neuronium's capabilities without forking the core repository.

1) Developer writes an MCP server (or reuses an existing one) exposing tools with JSON Schema. 2) Adds the endpoint + auth to neuronium.toml [[tools.mcp]]. 3) The first run discovers tools via the MCP handshake, registers them in McpToolRegistry, and makes them callable from any McpToolNode in the DAG. 4) For deeper customization, the developer ships a Python package with entry-points (group "neuronium_agent.nodes" / ".critics" / ".recovery_policies"); pip install in worker image automatically registers them. 5) The plan can then reference the new node type or policy by name; tracing/replay/critics work transparently.
A reachable MCP server endpoint with valid auth credentials, JSON Schema descriptions for each exposed tool, and an entry under [[tools.mcp]] in neuronium.toml.
The agent process starts (or a new tool is hot-registered) and performs the MCP handshake; an McpToolNode in the DAG invokes a registered tool by name with typed input matching its JSON Schema.

Tool descriptors SHALL be validated against JSON Schema, persisted in McpToolRegistry with their schema_artifact_id, and exposed to the planner; on invocation the system SHALL marshal input → MCP call → unmarshal output, write the response into CAS, emit MCP_TOOL_CALLED + MCP_TOOL_RESULT trace events with input/output artifact IDs, and apply timeout + retry per node config; schema-violating responses SHALL be rejected with a typed McpProtocolError.
tool_name (str), payload (dict matching tool input schema), node-level timeout, retry config; MCP server endpoint + auth from config.
tool_response (dict), output_artifact_id; MCP_TOOL_CALLED + MCP_TOOL_RESULT trace events; updated NodeStatus.
McpToolRegistry persisted in IndexStore; per-call leases or sessions managed by the MCP client; CAS holds input + output blobs.
Requirement
The MCP integration SHALL validate every tool input and output against the registered JSON Schema and SHALL reject mismatches with a typed McpProtocolError.
Tool calls SHALL be replayable: the recorded MCP_TOOL_RESULT artifact MUST be served verbatim during replay, and any live MCP call attempt in strict replay SHALL raise NetworkAttemptInReplayError.
Requirement
MCP overhead: marshalling/unmarshalling + registry lookup SHALL add ≤ 100 ms p95 per call (excluding the tool's own work and network).
Fill In

A Python package implementing a custom node type (NodeProtocol with type identifier "my-node") and/or a recovery policy (RecoveryPolicyProtocol), declared via entry-points in pyproject.toml: [project.entry-points."neuronium_agent.nodes"] my-node = "mypkg:MyNode"; package is pip-installed in the worker environment.

The agent process starts; the plugin loader scans entry-points and imports declared classes; the planner SHALL be allowed to emit DAG nodes of type "my-node" and recovery policies of registered names; on execution the executor instantiates the plugin class with the standard contract.

The plugin class SHALL be validated against the protocol (TypeError on mismatch), registered in the type registry, and become available to the planner; tracing, critics, replay, sandboxing and observability SHALL work transparently for the new type with no core code changes; a PLUGIN_LOADED trace event SHALL list each loaded plugin name + version + module digest at startup.
pyproject.toml entry-points block; installed Python package implementing the protocol; optional config block under [plugins.my-node] in neuronium.toml.
Type registry entries; PLUGIN_LOADED trace event; new node executions traced uniformly with built-in nodes.
In-memory plugin registry per process; persistent type-name → module digest mapping recorded for replay reproducibility.
Requirement

The plugin loader SHALL discover plugins via three entry-point groups (neuronium_agent.nodes / .critics / .recovery_policies) at startup and SHALL fail fast on any class that does not implement the declared protocol.
Replay SHALL verify that loaded plugin module digests match those recorded in the original run; a mismatch SHALL emit a PLUGIN_DIGEST_DRIFT warning and (in strict mode) raise PluginDriftError.
Custom nodes SHALL be subject to the same sandboxing rules as built-in nodes: code-executing custom nodes MUST run in the Docker sandbox with no host network unless explicitly whitelisted in config.
Requirement
Time-to-onboard a custom node from skeleton to integration test green SHALL be ≤ 1 day for a Python developer familiar with the project.
Plugin loading SHALL add ≤ 500 ms to process startup for up to 20 registered plugins.
Fill In

(a) CLI `neuronium-agent` (Click-based) for human operators; (b) Python API `neuronium_agent.api.create_runner()` / `AgentRunner` for programmatic integration; (c) optional rendered HTML artifact viewer for read-only inspection. No first-party Web/Telegram/Slack UI in scope; integrations are left to adopters via the public API and trace_event_listener callback.

CLI commands: `neuronium-agent run`, `neuronium-agent run --interactive`, `neuronium-agent run --trace-id <id>` (resume), `neuronium-agent run --replay <id>`, `neuronium-agent worker`, `neuronium-agent run -v` / `--summary`. Python API entry: `from neuronium_agent.api import create_runner`. Config entry: `neuronium.toml` + env var `NEURONIUM_OPENAI_API_KEY`.

Timeline view (default): plan → node-by-node progress → critic verdicts → final summary. Verbose view (-v): stdout/stderr per node + critic evidence. Summary view (--summary): execution_id, trace_id, totals, durations, terminal state. Interactive view: keystroke control (p=pause, s=stop, r=revise). Worker view: job-pull log, lease info.

Input: RunRequest JSON-compatible dict { objective: str, constraints: list[str], mode: "batch"|"supervised"|"interactive", metadata: dict }; ControlCommand { type, payload }; CLI flags. Output: RunHandle + RunStatus DTOs; trace files in jsonl|json|zip; artifacts addressed by canonical artifact_id (sha256-based); rendered HTML; structured logs.

Three entry-point groups for pip-installable plugins: `neuronium_agent.nodes`, `neuronium_agent.critics`, `neuronium_agent.recovery_policies`. MCP tools registered under `[[tools.mcp]]` in neuronium.toml. trace_event_listener callback passed via create_runner() for in-process observers. Custom embeddings provider via `[memory.embeddings]`. All extension points are versioned and module-digested for replay.
Fill In

(1) Orchestrator/Control Plane (in-process AgentRunner facade); (2) Planner Service (HTN → DAG); (3) Execution Engine (deterministic DAG executor); (4) Node Runtime (ModelNode, CodeNode in Docker sandbox, McpToolNode + custom plugin nodes); (5) Trace Service (recording + export + replay); (6) Verification Service (critics: demo, generic, business + custom plugin critics); (7) Memory Service (GraphRAG-lite ingest + vector kNN + graph expansion + retrieval); (8) Storage Service (BlobStore + IndexStore, multi-backend); (9) Queue Service (Redis + RQ workers, lease-based ownership); (10) Recovery Service (policies for TRANSIENT/PERSISTENT/SYSTEMIC/CRITICAL); (11) Plugin Loader (entry-point discovery for nodes/critics/recovery policies, module-digest pinning); (12) Observability Bus (trace_event_listener fan-out, structured-log correlation, /metrics endpoint hook); (13) MCP Client + Registry (tool discovery, JSON Schema validation, replay-safe invocation); (14) Sandbox Service (Docker isolation for CodeNode and unsafe custom nodes); (15) Config Service (TOML + env layered loader with priority and override).

Orchestrator: state machine, control protocol, checkpoints. Planner: produce typed DAG from objective+constraints. Executor: topological execution, retries, timeouts, determinism. Node Runtime: actual side-effect (LLM, code, MCP). Trace: append-only event log + replay. Verification: pre-commit critic gates with evidence. Memory: store/retrieve graph + vector context. Storage: persist blobs (CAS) and structured rows (runs/artifacts/events). Queue: distribute jobs across workers. Recovery: classify failures and decide retry/escalate.

Plan→Execute→Verify→Adapt loop: 1) AgentRunner.start writes a RUN row, calls Planner, persists DAG. 2) Executor walks the DAG, for each node it gathers inputs from CAS, calls Node Runtime, validates output via Critic, computes canonical artifact_id, writes blob + index rows, appends trace events. 3) Control commands hit the Orchestrator state machine; pause/stop write a checkpoint and gate further execution. 4) On failure, Recovery classifies and either retries or escalates; on critic FAIL, the node is rejected and revise/replan is suggested. 5) replay_run feeds recorded artifacts to nodes instead of live calls.

Public Python facade `AgentRunner` (Pydantic v2 DTOs): start(RunRequest, *, on_handle_ready=None) → RunHandle; get_status(handle) → RunStatus; control(handle, ControlCommand) → RunStatus; resume_run(trace_id) → RunHandle; export_trace(handle, format, path); replay(trace_id) → RunHandle; get_latest_pause_context(trace_id); read_artifact_json(artifact_id); get_trace_events(trace_id); get_latest_rendered_artifact_path(trace_id). Factory: create_runner(config=None, *, trace_event_listener=None). Internal contracts: BlobStore (put/get/exists by artifact_id), IndexStore (insert/query runs/artifacts/trace_events), NodeProtocol (run(inputs)->outputs), CriticProtocol (verify(outputs)->Verdict).

ControlCommand{ type: Literal["continue","pause","revise","replan","stop","escalate"], payload: dict[str,Any]={} }. InterruptRequest{ command: Literal["pause","stop"], mode: Literal["graceful","immediate"]="graceful", export_path: str|None }.

RunHandle{ trace_id: str, execution_id: str, created_at: datetime }. RunStatus{ state: Literal["PENDING","RUNNING","PAUSED","COMPLETED","FAILED","CANCELLED"], progress: float|None, current_node_ref: str|None, message: str|None }. NodeStatus{ status: Literal["PENDING","READY","RUNNING","COMPLETED","FAILED","TIMEOUT","RETRYING","CANCELLED"] }. FailureClass{ kind: Literal["TRANSIENT","PERSISTENT","SYSTEMIC","CRITICAL"], message: str, retryable: bool }. TraceExportFormat ∈ {"jsonl","json","zip"}.

Typed hierarchy in `neuronium_agent.errors`: ConfigError (bad/missing config), PlanningError (invalid HTN/DAG), ExecutionError (node-level, wraps FailureClass), ControlError (illegal state transition), TraceError (corrupt/missing event), StorageError (CAS/index IO), VerificationError (critic FAIL), ReplayError (incl. NetworkAttemptInReplayError, ArtifactMissingError). Strategy: fail fast at boundaries, classify via FailureClass, persist failure into trace, apply recovery policy (retry with backoff for TRANSIENT, escalate/halt for CRITICAL); never lose a partial checkpoint.

Level 0: User/CLI/API → AgentRunner → ConfigSnapshot persisted → Planner → DAG → Executor → {Node Runtime + Memory.retrieve + MCP Client + Critic chain + Recovery} → Storage{BlobStore CAS, IndexStore} → Trace → Observability Bus → Listeners. Level 1 per node: (a) Executor reads input artifact_ids from IndexStore, fetches blobs from CAS; (b) optional memory.retrieve(query) → MemoryRetrievalLog event; (c) Node Runtime invoked (LLM / Code in Docker sandbox / MCP tool / custom plugin); (d) raw output bytes captured, canonical_artifact_id = sha256 over canonical JSON computed; (e) blob written to CAS, Artifact row inserted, Node updated, TraceEvent appended — all transactional per phase boundary with fsync; (f) Critic chain (in priority order) verifies output, writes CriticVerdict + evidence; (g) on FAIL Recovery Service applies policy → retry / revise / escalate; (h) Observability Bus fan-outs every persisted event to listeners with at-least-once + circuit-breaker. Level 1 replay: identical pipeline but Node Runtime reads recorded output blob from CAS by recorded artifact_id; live network calls blocked; replay_index isolated. Level 1 ingest: Memory Service chunks source → embeds → writes MemoryNode + MemoryEdge + vector index transactionally → MEMORY_INGESTED event. Level 1 worker: RQ pulls job → claim JobLease(execution_id) → Orchestrator runs DAG → heartbeat extends lease → on terminal state release lease → on crash lease expires → another worker re-claims (idempotent because state lives in Postgres + CAS).

1) User objective + constraints (CLI / Python API / RunRequest). 2) neuronium.toml config (project, determinism, storage, llm, queue, verification, memory, sandbox, plugins, tools.mcp). 3) Environment variables / .env (NEURONIUM_OPENAI_API_KEY, DB URL, REDIS URL, NEURONIUM_LOG_LEVEL, etc.). 4) MCP tool servers (external HTTP/stdio endpoints with JSON Schema). 5) Web fetch tool (HTTP, sandboxed). 6) Recorded trace bundles (jsonl/json/zip — for replay or import). 7) Memory store (prior runs' artifacts + ingested docs + embeddings + graph). 8) Custom user-supplied plugins discovered via entry-points (nodes / critics / recovery_policies). 9) Embeddings provider (sentence-transformers locally or OpenAI / pluggable). 10) Secret store (env / Vault / cloud secret manager) for API keys never persisted to trace. 11) Pre-existing artifacts in CAS (e.g., reference data sets) referenced by node inputs.
Local/dev: 1 dev machine, Python 3.11+, 2 vCPU / 4 GB RAM / 10 GB disk; SQLite + FS CAS in `.neuronium/`; outbound HTTPS to OpenAI; optional Docker Desktop for CodeNode sandbox.

Production (small cluster): control-plane node 2 vCPU / 4 GB; 2–8 worker nodes 4 vCPU / 8 GB each (Docker engine on each for CodeNode); Postgres ≥14 with pgvector extension (4 vCPU / 16 GB / 200 GB SSD); Redis ≥6 (2 vCPU / 4 GB); shared object/blob storage or NFS for FS CAS (500 GB+); egress to OpenAI / MCP tool endpoints.

Software/runtime: Python ≥3.11; pydantic 2.x; click 8.x; openai 1.x–3.x; python-dotenv; beautifulsoup4; optional extras: psycopg (postgres), redis + rq (queue), pgvector, sentence-transformers + numpy (embeddings), docker SDK (sandbox).
Storage: BlobStore=fs_cas (content-addressable on-disk) and pluggable; IndexStore ∈ {sqlite, postgres}; migrations under `neuronium_agent/storage/migrations/`.
Networking: HTTPS egress to LLM provider; intra-cluster TCP to Postgres/Redis; Docker network isolation for CodeNode (no host network by default); MCP tool endpoints reachable from workers.
Observability: structured logs from CLI/API; trace events as the source of truth; pluggable trace_event_listener for Prometheus/Loki/OpenTelemetry adapters built by adopters.

Security: API keys via env / secret manager (never persisted to trace/artifacts); Docker sandbox for CodeNode (no host net, read-only mounts); Postgres role separation (RW for workers, RO for auditors); replay runs write only to a dedicated replay_index.

CI/CD: pytest + pytest-asyncio + coverage suite covering canonical JSON, config loading, storage, deterministic reproducibility; Hatchling build backend; pip-installable via extras `[docker], [postgres], [redis], [pgvector], [embeddings], [dev], [all]`.
Backups & DR: nightly pg_dump of IndexStore; CAS snapshotting (rsync/object-store versioning); trace bundles (zip) act as portable, self-verifying backups per run.

(a) Ingest of 1k chunks (~1 MB) ≤ 30 s p95 on a 4-vCPU host; (b) Idempotent on duplicate source (no duplicate MemoryNode by content sha256); (c) Embedding writes are transactional with row inserts (no dangling vectors on partial failure); (d) Retrieval p95 ≤ 200 ms for k=10 / hops=1 over 100k MemoryNode rows; (e) recall@10 ≥ 0.85 on benchmark dataset; (f) MEMORY_HIT records resolved IDs and scores so replay reproduces identical context regardless of live store changes; (g) Documented in docs and integration-tested with both sentence-transformers and OpenAI embeddings.

column2: Implement Memory schema (MemoryNode, MemoryEdge, MemoryRetrievalLog) with pgvector indexes on Postgres and sqlite-vec fallback on SQLite; migrations idempotent.
column4: Migrations apply cleanly to empty DB and to existing schema; vector index created with correct dim; sample insert + kNN query returns expected order on fixture data.

column2: Implement ingest pipeline: source chunking (configurable size + overlap), embeddings provider abstraction (sentence-transformers + OpenAI), content-sha256 dedup, transactional writes, MEMORY_INGESTED trace event with source_digest + counts.
column4: 1k-chunk ingest ≤ 30 s p95; re-ingest of same source produces zero new rows and emits MEMORY_INGESTED with status="duplicate"; partial-failure test rolls back cleanly (no dangling vectors).

column2: Implement retrieval pipeline: vector kNN search + graph expansion over MemoryEdge up to `hops`, dedup + rank by combined score, optional score_threshold, MEMORY_HIT/MISS trace event with resolved IDs; expose memory.retrieve as public API; integrate with executor so DAG nodes can declare a retrieval step.
column4: Retrieval p95 ≤ 200 ms (k=10, hops=1, 100k nodes) on Postgres+pgvector; recall@10 ≥ 0.85 on benchmark; replay test confirms identical context resolved from recorded MEMORY_HIT IDs even after live store mutated.

Implement the full Verification subsystem: CriticProtocol contract, three-source critic discovery (programmatic critics.register, [verification.critics] in TOML, entry-points group "neuronium_agent.critics"), in-process gate chain by priority, CriticVerdict persistence with evidence_artifact_id, CRITIC_VERDICT trace events, recovery policy revise_on_critic_fail with max_revise_attempts, CRITIC_REJECTED / REVISE_TRIGGERED / REVISE_GIVE_UP / ESCALATED event chain, failed-artifact retention indexed by node_ref + attempt index, ship built-in critics (demo, generic, business-rule template).
T-1 (executor + trace + artifacts), T-2 (control protocol — revise/escalate piggybacks on it).

(a) Critics discovered from all three sources; bad protocol implementation fails fast at startup with typed error; (b) Gate chain runs in priority order; first FAIL blocks downstream execution; (c) Critic gate overhead ≤ 20 ms p95 per critic per node (excluding the critic's own logic); (d) On FAIL with revise_on_critic_fail, planner regenerates affected sub-DAG using evidence as context; ≥ 80% of revisable failures converge to PASS within 3 attempts on QA benchmark; (e) Failed artifacts retained with attempt index, queryable and exportable; (f) ESCALATED state reached cleanly when max_revise_attempts exceeded; (g) Built-in critics shipped and documented.
